coredata.dc:title: The impact of Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) on the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 under UV and visible light
coredata.dc:creator: Mozdbar, Afsaneh
Nouralishahi, Amideddin
Fatemi, Shohreh
Talatori, Fatemeh Sadat
coredata.dc:description: In this study, the impact of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) on the photocatalytic performance of TiO2 in aqueous medium was investigated under UV and visible light. CQDs were synthesized through a solvothermal procedure, and the effect of synthesis conditions on their optical properties were optimized. The CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites were prepared via a hydrothermal method using CQDs with the extreme band-gaps (i.e. CQDs450 and CQDs550). The physicochemical properties of CQDs and CQDs/TiO2 samples were studied by various characterization techniques. According to the High-Resolution Tunneling Electron microscopy (HRTEM) micrographs, the synthesized spherical CQDs had diameters smaller than 5 nm. The Diffuse Reflectance Spectroscopy (DRS) analysis revealed that incorporation of CQDs into nanocomposite samples causes a remarkable shift in absorption edge to the visible region, in comparison with pure TiO2. According to the experimental data, the nanocomposite sample containing CQDs with the higher absorption edge, i.e. CQDs550/TiO2, showed a superior performance in photocatalytic removal of methylene blue (MB) under visible light. In this regard, the removal percent of MB on CQDs550/TiO2 and CQDs450/TiO2 nanocomposites, with 4%wt CQDs, under visible light (λ > 420 nm) were 92.1 % and 53.4 % after 120 min of irradiation at pH = 11 and 150 mgcat./L catalyst dosage, which are respectively 2.4 and 1.4 times higher than that on pure TiO2. However, under UV irradiation the observed photocatalytic performances were in the reverse order. Finally, two different reaction mechanisms have been proposed to explain the obtained results on CQDs/TiO2 samples, based on possible electron transfer between TiO2 and CQDs nanoparticles under UV and Visible lights.

head.ce:title: The impact of Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) on the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 under UV and visible light

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, the impact of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) on the photocatalytic performance of TiO2 in aqueous medium was investigated under UV and visible light. CQDs were synthesized through a solvothermal procedure, and the effect of synthesis conditions on their optical properties were optimized. The CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites were prepared via a hydrothermal method using CQDs with the extreme band-gaps (i.e. CQDs450 and CQDs550). The physicochemical properties of CQDs and CQDs/TiO2 samples were studied by various characterization techniques. According to the High-Resolution Tunneling Electron microscopy (HRTEM) micrographs, the synthesized spherical CQDs had diameters smaller than 5 nm. The Diffuse Reflectance Spectroscopy (DRS) analysis revealed that incorporation of CQDs into nanocomposite samples causes a remarkable shift in absorption edge to the visible region, in comparison with pure TiO2. According to the experimental data, the nanocomposite sample containing CQDs with the higher absorption edge, i.e. CQDs550/TiO2, showed a superior performance in photocatalytic removal of methylene blue (MB) under visible light. In this regard, the removal percent of MB on CQDs550/TiO2 and CQDs450/TiO2 nanocomposites, with 4%wt CQDs, under visible light (λ > 420 nm) were 92.1 % and 53.4 % after 120 min of irradiation at pH = 11 and 150 mgcat./L catalyst dosage, which are respectively 2.4 and 1.4 times higher than that on pure TiO2. However, under UV irradiation the observed photocatalytic performances were in the reverse order. Finally, two different reaction mechanisms have been proposed to explain the obtained results on CQDs/TiO2 samples, based on possible electron transfer between TiO2 and CQDs nanoparticles under UV and Visible lights.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Methylene blue (MB) is a common and inexpensive chemical dye which is widely used in the textile, medical and dental industries. However, because of its aromatic structure, it often has several side effects and risks for the environment and human health, making it indispensable to degradation of dyes from wastewater through technologies like photocatalytic processes [1–3]. Being activated in chemical reactions, photocatalysts, as a kind of semiconductors, are capable to generate electron-hole pairs under light irradiation, having high enough energetic photons [3,4]. Among all known photocatalysts, TiO2 is one of the most well-known and active candidates; but it is only activated under ultraviolet (UV) light due to its rather large band gap of 3.2 eV [5]. This is considered as one of the biggest problems of TiO2, causing its lack of catalytic activity in visible (vis) light. Therefore, today a lot of research is focused on improving the activity of TiO2 in visible light.

Recently, there have been different techniques for improving photocatalytic activity of TiO2 under visible light; like doping with various atoms such as S [6,7], Ag [8,9], N [10] P [11,12], B [13,14], compositions such as Bi2O4 [15], Fe2O3 [16], CdS [17], and recently graphene [18], and g-C3N4 [19], etc. It is observed that electronegative property of these atoms may lead to changing the band gap of TiO2 nanoparticles. Making nanocomposite with different materials particularly carbon nanostructures are another efficient method to enhance photocatalytic performance of TiO2 [2,20]. Lei et al. studied photodegradation of MB by CdS/CNT-TiO2 composite [21]. They reported %98 removal of MB under UV. Nevertheless, the composite photocatalyst only degraded %35 under visible light. Although carbon structures do not have direct effect on the performance of the active sites, they may facilitate the photocatalytic mechanism through improving the conductivity and structure of photocatalyst, which are important to prevent the recombination of electron-hole pairs [1,22]. In 2016, TiO2/carbon composite was synthesized by Maletic´ et al. via a hydrothermal method for degradation of MB [23]. They used glucose and titanium isopropoxide as precursors of carbon and titanium, respectively. Zhao et al. synthesized TiO2-Graphene nanocomposite via a sol-gel method to be used in photocatalytic degradation of MB under UV and visible light irradiation [24]. They reported that their synthesized nanocomposite can degrade almost %88 of methylene blue under UV. However, the photocatalytic activity is dramatically reduced under Visible-light, as only %52 of initial MB concentration was removed from the solution.

Quantum dots (QDs) are known as semiconductor particles with a size below 10 nm, such as CdSe, GaAs, PbS, etc., which may be used for improving photocatalytic performance of TiO2 active sites [25]. However, their high toxicity and inevitable consequences on animals and human lives are considered as the main issue of mineral quantum dots [26]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a new class of nanoscale carbon materials that have recently attracted many attentions due to their low toxicity, proper bio-stability, unique surface state and optical properties, good solubility in water, wide absorption width, low cost, and high stability [27,28]. Today, these zero-dimension materials have many applications in different fields such as drug delivery, bioimaging [29–31].

To date, there are only a limited number of studies addressing the photocatalytic properties of CQDs [32–35]. Yu et al. prepared carbon quantum dots (CQDs)/TiO2 nano-sheet (TNS) composites as the photocatalyst for degradation of Rhodamine B under visible light [36]. They attributed the higher photocatalytic performance of CQDs/TNS nanocomposite to an up-converted photoluminescence behavior of CQDs nanoparticles. This phenomenon was also reported by other researchers [35,37]. Jin et al. examined methylene blue degradation on CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposite under visible light irradiation [38]. They reported that The NCQDs nanoparticles can increase the absorption of visible light on composite support and enhance the photocatalytic activity under visible light.

Unfortunately, most of the studies about the impact of CQDs on the performance of TiO2 photocatalyst focused solely on the photocatalytic activity of CQDs/TiO2 composites under visible light, with inadequate attention paid to the reaction mechanism. In the current study, we aim to investigate the impact of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) on the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 nanoparticles under both UV and visible light irradiation. We examined the photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue (MB) on CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposite under UV and visible light and proposed a mechanism based on possible electron transfer between CODs and TiO2 nanoparticles to explain the experimental data. The CQDs nanoparticles were synthesized through a solvothermal route, and the synthesis parameters were. The CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites was prepared by hydrothermal method and their photocatalytic performances were investigated under both ultraviolet and visible light. In addition, two proposed mechanisms were proposed for photocatalytic activity under both ultraviolet and visible light.

Citric acid (CA) (>%99.9, C6H8O7), monoethanolamine (MEA) (>%98, C2H7NO) and ethanol (%99, C2H5OH) were purchased from Merck Inc. and used without further purification. TiO2 powders (%99, anatase/rutile) was purchased from US Research Nanomaterials, Inc. Methylene blue (MB) (%97, C16H18ClN3S) was provided from Samchun Inc.

In the current study, several CQDs nanoparticles were synthesized via a solvothermal procedure at different conditions (see Table 1 ). Briefly, citric acid (CA), as the carbon precursor, was mixed well with 23 mL MEA - as the nitrogen precursor for N-doping process. After 10 min of vigorous stirring, the solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and was heated through a solvothermal route to produce CQDs samples. In order to investigate the effect of synthesis parameters on the CQDs' optical properties, the synthesis conditions have been selected using Design of Expert Software via full factorial method (see fig. S1 and table S1).
The resulted CQDs samples have been named based on their estimated absorption edges. For instance, CQDs 550 refer to CQDs sample which its estimated absorption edge is 550 nm.

To improve the photocatalytic performance of TiO2, the CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites were prepared via a hydrothermal route. For the nanocomposite preparation, 0.4 g of TiO2 powder was dispersed in 30 mL of ethanol under ultrasonic condition for 1 h. Then, the suspension of TiO2 was added to an aqueous solution of CQDs and was stirred for 3 h. The resulted mixture was then transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated up to 180 °C for 6 h. The suspension was washed with distilled water and ethanol and centrifuged several times to eliminate unreacted species. The final product was dried in an oven at 60 °C for 10 h.

The photocatalytic activity of the prepared samples in degradation reaction of Methylene blue (MB) was studied under ultraviolet and visible light. First, 0.1 g of the photocatalyst powder was well suspended with 100 mL of an aqueous solution of MB (10 mg/L) magnetically. The suspension was vigorously stirred for 30 min in a dark box to achieve the adsorption-desorption equilibrium condition. The reaction, then, was conducted under ultraviolet radiation of a 125 W mercury lamp, located 12 cm above the solution, for 120 min. The residual MB concentration during the reaction was determined at 664 nm by UV–visible spectrometer (UVmini-1240 SHIMADZU) every 10 min to investigate the photocatalytic behavior over the time. Then the removal percentage was calculated using UV–visible spectroscopy by the following formula Eq. (1), where Rp is removal percentage, C0 stands for initial concentration and C is the concentration for each time, measured from the absorbance and calibration curve of methylene blue [3,39,40].

To investigate the photocatalytic performance of the synthesized samples under visible light, the reaction performed at the same conditions explained above, while a UV filter was placed directly under the lamp.

The light absorption behavior of as-prepared CQDs and CQDs/TiO2 samples were investigated by UV–Visible spectroscopy using UVmini-1240 SHIMADZU. High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope (HRTEM) images were taken by Philips-30 cm with an acceleration voltage of 100kv to observe the particle size of CQDs. The surface functional groups on the synthesized samples were determined by Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) using Perkin-Elmer Spectrum RX I (RX-1). The emission behavior of CQDs was studied by photoluminescence (PL) spectra using Avaspec-2048-TEC. Diffuse Reflectance Spectroscopy (DRS) data were recorded using Avaspec-2048-TEC spectrometer. Brunauer-Emmertt-Teller (BET) surface areas of TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposite were determined by BELSORP MINI VAC II. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (XPS) was taken by ESCALAB 250 XPS to distinguish the chemical species in CQDs and CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposite. The field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) images were taken using Hitachi-S4160 to illustrate the morphology of TiO2 particles and CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposite.

Fig. 1a shows the results of absorption spectra in the visible-ultraviolet range of CQDs samples synthesized at different conditions. The absorption edges of the CQDs nanoparticles have been calculated from interception of the tangent line of absorption curves and x-axis, reported in Table 1. According to the tabulated results in Table 1, the absorption edge values of the synthesized CQDs samples lie in the range of 450 nm to 550 nm, which is in good accordance with the data reported in the literature using hydrothermal procedure. In this regard, Dey et al. synthesized several nitrogen-doped CQDs nanoparticles by hydrothermal method with absorption edge values between 250 and 350-nm [41]. In another study, Liang et al., described a hydrothermal route for synthesis of CQDs samples with absorption wavelength of 350 nm [42].

To study the effect of CQDs absorption edge on the photocatalytic activity of CQDs/TiO2, the CQDs samples with the highest and lowest absorption edge, i.e. CQDs550 and CQDs 450, have been applied for the synthesis of CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites. In this regard, the characterization results of mentioned samples have been presented in Fig. 1b-e. The HRTEM photogram of CQDs550 are depicted in Fig. 1b. As it can be observed the diameter of the synthesized carbon dots lays under 5 nm, which is the typical range of photoluminescence carbon nanoparticles [43,44].

The surface functional groups of CQDs550 and CQDs450 have been identified by Furrier Transform Infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) in Fig. 1c. In the FTIR pattern of CQDs550, the absorption peaks between 3200 and 3600 cm−1 may be attributed to OH and NH groups on the surface of CQDs550 [45,46]. The twin peaks in 2850–3000 cm−1 range are related to the symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibration of CH bond [47]. The absorption peaks centered at 1561 cm−1 and 1656 cm−1 can be tailored to stretching vibration of CC and CO respectively [47,48]. Moreover, the peaks at 1159 cm−1 and 1056 cm−1 may be related to bending vibration of CO and CH bond [47,49]. Finally, the peaks at 881 cm−1, 1056 cm−1 and 1397 cm−1 be related to the vibrations of NH [48], CN [50] and NO [45,51] bonds, respectively. As it can be observed in Fig. 1c, the FTIR spectrum of CQDs450 shows the peaks related to CO, CC, CH and OH bonds, similar to what observed in CQD550. However, the absence of the peaks of nitrogen containing groups, i.e. the peaks related to the vibration of CH, CN and NH2 bonds, is probably due to the absence of MEA, as the source of nitrogen, during the synthesis procedure.

In Fig. 1d and e, the emission behavior of CQDs450 and CQDs550 samples were investigated by PL analysis at four different excitation wavelengths, i.e. 350, 450, and 550 nm. As it can be seen, the emission behavior of the both samples is dependent to the excitation wavelength, which is probably due to the irradiative electron recombination as a result of distribution of particle size, and the abundance of surface functional groups on the surface of CQDs samples [52]. In fact, the presence of functional groups on CQDs may act as energy traps on the surface of CQDs nanoparticles, leading to a red shift in the resulted emission spectra.

In order to investigate the chemical species on the surface of synthesized samples, the results of X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) have been presented in Fig. 2 . In TiO2 spectrum, the peaks at 530.3 and 533.1 eV are related to binding energy of O1s photoelectrons [53]. While peaks at 458.3 and 463.9 eV are ascribed to Ti2p [37,53]. The presence of a small peak of C1s at 285.1 eV has been also reported by other researches [54] and may be related to carbon species remained in TiO2 structure during the synthesis procedure. The XPS profile of CQDs-550 nanoparticles show peaks between 284.2 and 287.9 eV and 398.7–401.6 eV which are related to C1s and N1s photoelectrons, respectively [55]. The peaks of N1s in CQDs-550 sample originated from MEA used in the synthesis procedure, confirming successful nitrogen-doping into CQDs structure. The peaks related to O, C, and Ti species in TiO2-CQDs550 pattern show their incorporation within the sample structure. However, the presence of N1s peak in TiO2-CQDs550 spectrum confirms the successful insertion of CQDs nanoparticles into TiO2 structure.

To investigate the morphology of TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles, Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy (FESEM) images were taken from the samples. The Fig. 3a shows that the pure TiO2 nanoparticles were relatively uniform with small spherical-shaped particles. The FESEM image of CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposite is illustrated in Fig. 3b. According to the FESEM data, the presence of CQDs in the nanocomposite sample did not have remarkable effects on the morphology and size of CQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles, although it seems that the surface roughness and the porosity were slightly increased in the presence of CQDs nanoparticles.

The light absorption behavior of TiO2 nanoparticles in the presence and absence of CQDs nanoparticles are reported in Fig. 4 . The results of UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectra of the prepared photocatalysts have been presented in Fig. 4a. As it is observed, the amounts of absorption edges of the nanocomposite samples are shifted to higher wavelength than that of bare TiO2 nanoparticles, showing a remarkable decrease in band gap energy due to CQDs incorporation in the nanocomposites' structures. In order to determine the the band gap energies, the amounts of Kubelka-Munk function of the prepared samples have been calculated from Eq. (2), where R stands for Reflection values.
(2)

The amounts of band gap energies can be estimated from Tauc plot (Fig. 4b) in which “h” is Plank constant and “ν” is the frequency of the incident photon. Just at the threshold of the absorption region, where a linear behavior is observed in Tauc plot, the intercept of the extrapolated tangent line with the horizontal axis gives the band gap energy [56]. According to the results presented in Fig. 4b, bare TiO2 nanoparticles has a band gap energy (Eg) of 3.22 eV which is in a good agreement with the data reported in the literature [57,58]. However, the amounts of optical band gap energy (Eg) remarkably reduce to 2.12 eV and 2.51 eV due to the incorporation of low- band gap CQDs nanoparticle in CQDs550/TiO2 and CQDs450/TiO2 samples, respectively. In addition, the Tauc plot in nanocomposite samples presents two linear regions, unlike the bare TiO2 nanoparticles. This observation is along with the behavior reported by Akhavan et al. and can be attributed to the presence of different photo-active chemical species (i.e., CQDs and TiO2) in the nanocomposites' structure [59]. In this regard, the larger Eg in CQDs550/TiO2 and CQDs450/TiO2 samples, falls between 3.2 eV–3.3 eV, are due to the presence of TiO2 in the nanocomposites' structures.

In order to investigate the porous structure of the synthesized samples, the nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms have been reported in Fig. 5a. As it can be clearly observed, the shape of the isotherms for all the synthesized photocatalysts are in good agreement with Type IV IUPAC isotherms, indicating a well-developed mesoporous structure [60]. The sharp adsorption step at the P/P0 > 0.8 and the presence of a hysteresis loop at P/P0 > 0.7 indicate that the synthesized samples have a well-defined structure with relatively regular meso-pores [61]. In addition, the observed hysteresis loops in Fig. 5a are very similar to IUPAK loop type H4, implying slit-shaped pores. The pore size distribution diagrams of the synthesized samples have been provided in Fig. 5b. According to the presented diagrams, the incorporation of CQDs nanoparticles in nanocomposite structures causes a remarkable increase in pore diameters which is consistent with larger hysteresis loops of nanocomposite samples in Fig. 5a, compared to that of bare TiO2. The amounts of specific surface areas, average pore diameters, and pore volumes have been tabulated in Table 2 . Although the results express that the presence of CQDs nanoparticles has almost no significant effect on the overall specific surface area, the pore volume and the average pore diameter have been increased up to 1.34 and 3.2 times in the presence of CQDs nanoparticles. This observation is in a good agreement with the adsorption data in Fig. 5. The larger pore diameter in CQDs550/TiO2, nanocomposite, compared to that of TiO2, can make surface active sites to be accessible more efficiently during photocatalytic reactions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic performance under visible and UV light
ce:section.ce:para: In order to study the promoting effect of CQDs on the photocatalytic performance of TiO2, the CQDs samples with the highest absorption edge, CQDs550, and the lowest absorption edge, CQDs450, were applied for modification of titanium oxide. To investigate photodegradation reaction of methylene blue on the synthesized samples, the absorption UV–vis spectrum at the characteristic wavelength (664 nm) has been monitored to determine the residual MB concentration during the reaction (See fig. S2).

The photocatalytic performance of CQDs550/TiO2 and CQDs450/TiO2 nanocomposites in removal of methylene blue under visible light has been presented in Fig. 6 .a. The decrease of MB concentration in the initial 30 min, when the light is off, is due to adsorption of MB on the photocatalysts surface. As it can be observed, the adsorption capacity of all three samples are identical, however the minor differences may be attributed to the effect of CQDs on the morphological properties of TiO2 (see Table 2) and the difference in surface wettability of the samples.

Fig. 6.a, shows the effect of CQDs on photocatalytic performance of TiO2 under visible light. As it is also presented in Table 3 , after 120 min of exposure to visible light irradiation, the CQDs550/TiO2 and CQDs450/TiO2 nanocomposites showed a removal percentage of 83 % and 42 %, while the removal percentage by TiO2 only reaches to 36 %. The rather low photocatalytic performance of pure TiO2 under visible light is also reported by other researchers and can be attributed to its large band gap (3.2 eV) [62,63]. In fact, there are numerous attempts in the literature aiming to enhance the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 nanoparticles under visible light by incorporating different species into TiO2 structure to improve its band gap [61–65].

The results reported in Fig. 6a demonstrated that the addition of CQDs nanoparticles, with lower band gap compared to TiO2 nanoparticles, to nanocomposites' structure can cause a significant increase in overal photocatalytic activity under visible light. In this regard, the difference between the photocatalytic activities of CQDs550/TiO2 and CQDs450/TiO2 may be attributed to the lower bandgap of CQDs550 nanoparticles compared to CQDs450, due possibly to the presence of nitrogen atoms in CQDs550. In fact, the higher excitation wavelength of CQDs550, i.e. 550 nm, may describe the superior activity CQDs550/TiO2 under visible light.

By comparing the data in Fig. 6b, it surprisingly seems that the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 nanoparticles under UV reduced upon incorporation of CQDs nanoparticles in the nanocomposite samples, in opposite to what was observed under visible irradiation.

According to the reported results in Table 3, after 120 min of irradiation of UV light, 95 % of MB was removed from the solution in the presence of TiO2 nanoparticles. However, the MB removal only reaches to 85 % and 77 % in CQDs450/TiO2 and CQDs550/TiO2, respectively.

As it is observed in Fig. 6, the CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites show superior photocatalytic activity under visible light compared to pure TiO2, while their photocatalytic performance was impaired under UV irradiation. In addition, it seems that the higher amounts of CQDs' band-gap result in more extreme effect on the photocatalytic activity of CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites in both visible and UV range. Proposing a mechanism to describe such a behavior of CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites through both visible and UV range can be a challenging task.

In aqueous medium, the photocatalytic degradation of MB on pure TiO2 under UV irradiation may be described by Eqs. (1) to (12) [66,67]. Briefly, when pure TiO2 nanoparticles are irradiated under high energy UV beams (λ < 390 nm), Electron-Hole pairs are formed on active sites in a reversible process (Eqs. (1) and (2)). Then, the adsorbed water molecules on TiO2 may react with the resulted holes to produce hydroxyl radicals and H+ ions (Eq. (3)), while dissolved oxygen can be adsorbed on excited sites on TiO2 to form superoxide radical (i.e. O2 − ads), which may undergo several reactions, producing surface hydroxyl radicals (TiO2-OHads) and OH− ion through Eqs. (4)–(9). The OH− ions also may react with holes on TiO2 to form TiO2-OHads via an additional step (reaction 10). Finally, the adsorbed methylene blue molecules may be decomposed by surface superoxide or hydroxyl groups (TiO2-O2 − ads, TiO2-OHads) through reactions 11 and 12.

The suggested mechanism of photodegradation process is initiated by the excitation of TiO2 under UV light (Reaction 1). Regarding the large bandgap of pure TiO2, the Electro-Hole pairs are not able to be formed effectively under low energy visible light, hindering the photocatalytic activity of pure TiO2 in visible range (Fig. 7a). However, the incorporation of CQDs, with small band gap, can enhance the performance of TiO2 under visible light irradiation through improving the electronic transfer within the valence and conduction bands of the CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites. Fig. 7b, illustrates a possible electron transfer mechanism within the nanocomposite structures under visible light. In visible wavelength region, the electrons in the valence band of CQDs nanoparticles, not TiO2, may be excited to the CQDs conduction band via a reversible process (reactions 13 and 14). The water molecules may adsorb on CQDs hole (reaction 15), and then react with MB molecules through reaction 16. The excited electrons in CQDs structure (see reaction 13), may transfer to the empty conduction band of unexcited TiO2 sites via reaction 17 (Fig. 11a) to enable them to participate in oxidative reactions (reactions 4–9). Finally, adsorbed methylene blue may be decomposed by surface OHads and O2 − ads species as described in reactions 11 and 12. As a result, the extra electrons provided on TiO2 active sites through the described mechanism, may lead a significant improvement of the photocatalytic performance in visible light [68].

The possible electron transfer process in UV range is demonstrated in Fig. 7c. Under UV irradiation, the electrons in conduction bands of both CQDs and TiO2 nanoparticles are simultaneously excited to their valence bands (Reactions 1 and 13). The exited electrons may then undergo an interparticle recombination process (Fig. 7c) via reactions 18 and 19. As the result, the probability of electron recombination process in CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposite may enhance via electron transfer between the valence and conduction bands of CQDs and TiO2 through reactions 2, 14, 18 and 19, explaining the lower the photocatalytic activity of CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposite under UV light, compared to pure TiO2.

As can be seen from the proposed mechanism, the photocatalytic performance of CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites under visible and UV lights is significantly influenced by the electron transfer from carbon quantum dots to TiO2 nanoparticles (reactions 17 and 18). This is in a good accordance with the reports by other researchers on TiO2 nanocomposites with carbon quantum dots [69,70] although carbon can also function as an electron acceptor in TiO2 composites with other carbon nanoparticles, like reduced graphene oxide [71].

In order to gain a better understanding of the real MB degradation mechanism, the results of scavenging experiment have been shown in Fig. 8 to study the role of active species (i.e., O2 −, OH, and h+) in the photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue. The experiments were conducted in the presence of scavengers including methyl viologen (MV2+) for detection of O2 − [72], ethanol (EtOH) for detection of OH [73], and ascorbic acid as the hole scavenger [74]. The photocatalytic degradation experiments were conducted at pH = 9, and catalyst dosage of 150 mgcat./L under visible light irradiation for 120 min of reaction time. The obtained results showed that removal percentage of methylene blue dramatically dropped in the presence of scavengers. According to the presented data in Fig. 8, the largest decrease has been observed in the presence of MV2+, which indicts that O2 − may be the main active component during the MB photodegradation reaction on both CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposite samples. However, the higher concentration of methylene blue in the presence of Ascorbic acid show that holes do not participate in MB degradation as efficiently as oxygen containing active species. Therefore, based on our proposed reaction mechanism (reactions 1 to 19), it appears that electron transfer from the excited CQDs conduction band to TiO2 conduction band (reaction 17) is a critical step in promoting the MB photodegradation reaction under visible light, because it can accelerate MB degradation reaction by improving O2 − formation on TiO2 conduction bands. However, Regarding the fact that h+ does not play an essential role in MB photodegradation, it seems that formation of OHads radicals on the existed holes in TiO2 or CQDs (reaction 10 and 15) has a minor role in MB degradation under visible light.

The results of photocatalytic activity of the composite samples in MB degradation under visible light at different CQDs concentrations (2 wt%, 4 wt%, 6 wt%) and pH 9 are reported in Fig. 9 . According to the represented data, the best photocatalytic performance at pH 9 is obtained at the CQDs concentration of 4 wt% in both the CQDs550/TiO2 and the CQDs450/TiO2 samples (83 % and 42 % MB removal respectively). In fact, the number of exited electrons at lower CQDs concentrations is not enough to efficiently transfer to the valence band of TiO2 nanoparticles. However, at excessive CQDs' concentrations, the TiO2 nanoparticles' active sites may be covered by CQDs nanoparticles, leading to a decline in overall photocatalytic activity.

In order to study the impact of pH on the photocatalytic performance of the optimum CQDs550/TiO2 and CQDs450/TiO2 nanocomposites, the results of the photocatalytic degradation of MB under visible light at various pH amounts have been represented in Fig. 10 . In this regard, the initial pH of the reaction mixture was adjusted by NaOH and HCl in the range of 3 to 11 (3, 5, 7, 9 and 11). As seen here, the highest MB degradation performance on CQDs550/TiO2 and CQDs450/TiO2 samples was 92.1 % and 53.4 % respectively at pH = 11. Increasing the photocatalytic activities with pH of the solution is in a good accordance with the reports by other researchers and may be attributed to the role of OH− ions in the photocatalytic oxidation reaction of methylene blue. In fact, OH− ions can react with the photogenerated holes (reaction 10) and slow down the recombination process [38].

The results of photodegradation reaction of methylene blue at different amounts of CQDs550/TiO2 and CQDs450/TiO2 photocatalysts (with 4 wt% CQDs) under visible light irradiation has been illustrated in Fig. 11 . Before each experiment, the initial pH of the solution has been adjusted at 11 and the initial concentration of MB was controlled at 10 mg/L. As it can be observed in Fig. 11, the degradation rate of MB has been sharply improved with increasing the photocatalysts' concentration. However, beyond optimum concentration of 150 mgcat./L the degradation rate starts to decline. This observation is in good accordance with the reports on the literature and may be attributed to the agglomeration of photocatalysts nanoparticles or reduced light translucency at excessive concentrations of photocatalysts [75].

Several studies have been conducted to enhance the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 nanoparticles. Table 4 compares some of the key information regarding the effects of different additives on the methylene blue degradation reaction on TiO2 photocatalyst. Comparing the tabulated data in Table 4, reveals that CQD's nanoparticles significantly improve TiO2's photocatalytic performance when exposed to visible light.

In this work, the effect of CQDs nanoparticles was investigated on the photocatalytic activity of CQD/TiO2 nanocomposites. The CQDs nanoparticles were synthesized via solvothermal method. The CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites were synthesized through a hydrothermal procedure using CQDs nanoparticles with extreme Bandgaps. According to the characterization data, the presence of CQDs had little effect on the surface and morphology of the nanocomposite samples, whereas it improved the overall optical properties. As a result of the incorporation of CQDs nanoparticles, the bandgap TiO2 shifts from 3.22 to 2.51 and 2.12 in CQDs450/TiO2 and CQDs550/TiO2 respectively. The photocatalytic experiments show that the incorporation of the CQDs into the nanocomposite samples improve the overall photocatalytic performance in MB degradation reaction under visible light. In contrast, the CQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles shows insufficient photocatalytic activity, comparing to pure TiO2, in the presence of CQDs under UV irradiation. According to the proposed mechanism, the exited electrons in CQDs structure under visible light may provide the requited electrons for photocatalytic MB degradation. However, the inferior performance of the composite samples under UV irradiation, in comparison with the pure TiO2, can be explained by inter-particle recombination process between CQDs and TiO2 nanoparticles.
